title: Law Enforcement Officer and Firefighter Recreation Pass Act
short_title: Law Enforcement Officer and Firefighter Recreation Pass Act
stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.
plain_summary: This bill gives free passes to law enforcement officers and firefighters so they can visit national parks and other federal recreation areas without paying fees. Right now, military members and their families already get these free passes. The bill explains who counts as a law enforcement officer or firefighter to get this benefit. These passes would be good for the entire lifetime of the person who gets them.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Law Enforcement Officer and Firefighter Recreation Pass Act.

sections.title: Section 2 National Recreational Passes for Law Enforcement Officers and Firefighters
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Federal Lands Recreation Enhancement Act. The change updates the rules about who can get a free annual pass to visit national parks and other federal recreation areas. Under the new rules, the government must give free annual passes to members of the Armed Forces and their family members who depend on them for support. The government must also give free annual passes to law enforcement officers and firefighters. These people must show adequate proof that they qualify for the free pass. The government will decide what kind of proof is needed. The law explains what these terms mean. A firefighter is any person who works for the federal government, a state government, a local government, or an Indian tribe and whose job involves putting out fires, including wildfires. A law enforcement officer is any officer, agent, or employee who works for the federal government, a state government, a local government, or an Indian tribe. This person must be authorized by law or by a government agency to prevent crimes, detect crimes, investigate crimes, or supervise people who have been sentenced for crimes.
